titleAr: يا عين ما لك لا تبكين تسكابا ؟
titleEn: O eye, why do you not weep

verses.sadr: يا عين ما لك لا تبكين تسكابا ؟
verses.ajuz: اذ راب دهر وكان الدهر ريابا
أو يسلبوا ، دون صف القوم ، أسلابا
مأوى الضريك اذا ما جاء منتابا

verses.sadr: يهدي الرعيل إذا ضاق السبيل بهم ،

verses.ajuz: ان هاب معضلة سنى لها بابا
لاقى الوغى لم يكن للموت هيابا

translationEn: O my eye, why do you not pour forth tears in torrents, when treacherous time has turned against us and time has ever been full of treachery? Weep for your brother on behalf of orphans and a widow, and weep for your brother when you dwell among strangers. Weep for your brother on behalf of horses that roamed like flocks of sand-grouse, for they lost their freedom and their spoils when he was laid low. A swift horse with strong hindquarters used to carry him, cloaked in the darkness of night like a garment. He would ride until morning against peoples who warred against him, or they would be stripped of their possessions before the ranks of men. He was the perfect youth, the protector of what was dear, a shelter for the needy when they came seeking him. He guided the vanguard when the path grew narrow for them, a broad-chested hero fit to mount even the most difficult affairs. Glory was his garment, generosity his nature, and truth his reserve — when his equal feared him. An eloquent speaker in great assemblies, a dispeller of darkness, whenever a difficult matter arose he lit its way. A bearer of banners, a crosser of valleys, a witness to councils, ever seeking blood-vengeance. A poison to enemies, a ransomer of captives, when he met battle he was no man to fear death.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pouring in torrents (of tears)
treacherous, full of treachery
the destitute, the needy person
one who comes repeatedly seeking help
the vanguard, the front rank of horsemen
what he holds dear, his honor and family
dispeller of darkness / injustice
captives, prisoners of war

context: This elegy is one of Al-Khansa's most celebrated poems mourning her brother Sakhr ibn Amr, who was a brave warrior and generous chieftain of the Sulaym tribe. She composed it after his death in battle, cataloguing his virtues as a protector of orphans, widows, and the needy, and as a fearless leader in war.

titleEn: A vast wilderness like worn garments

verses.ajuz: مخوف رداه ما يقيم به ركب
اذا حط عنها كورها جمل صعب

verses.sadr: يعاتبها في بعض ما أذنبت له ،
و قد جعلت في نفسها ان تخافه
verses.ajuz: وليس لها منه سلام ولا حرب

verses.sadr: فطرت بها ، حتى إذا اشتد ظمؤها ،

verses.ajuz: وجاء إلى أفياء ما علق الركب
ليكسب مجدا ، أو يحور لها نهب

translationEn: A vast, frightening wilderness like the worn-out lining of a shirt, whose perils prevent any caravan from settling within it. He crossed it on a swift camel that, when its load was removed, was like a recalcitrant male camel. It would reproach her for some imagined fault and strike her at times, though she had done nothing wrong. She had made it her nature to fear him, though between them was neither peace nor outright war. He rode her until her thirst grew intense and the people longed to rest and drink. He alighted her at a palm tree, abused but not domesticated, its supporting roots twisted and its branches fresh. He hung his sword and cloak from its branch and came to rest in the shade of whatever the caravan had hung. He dozed briefly, then leapt up with his saddle to earn glory or return with plunder. She bolted, racing a noble stallion returning from the herd, with a long cheek-strap and a broad chest.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: vast wilderness, barren expanse
worn-out, tattered (clothes or exhausted camels)
vast, desolate wilderness
swift in returning, fast-moving
the camel's saddle
thirst, the interval between waterings
shadows, shade
a noble horse descended from the famous stallion Ajuz

context: This poem describes a daring desert crossing undertaken by Sakhr, Al-Khansa's brother, praising his fearlessness and determination as he traversed a terrifying wilderness and rested briefly before riding off again in pursuit of glory and spoils.

titleAr: يا ابن الشريد ، على تنائي بيننا ،
titleEn: O son of al-Sharid, despite the distance between us

verses.sadr: يا ابن الشريد ، على تنائي بيننا ،

translationEn: O son of al-Sharid, despite the distance between us, may you be greeted, without disgrace, by one who reveres you. He was cheerful amid the finest provision when a grey morning came cutting through worn tent-ropes. Fragrant of embrace, slender, what a fine young man — easy-mannered with both kinsmen and strangers. A protector of honor, you would think him at war a lion of Bisha with bared fangs. A lion that companions warned each other about, massive and powerful, rough-pawed and lean-flanked. If you have perished, you lived as a true noble — pure in character and fine in garment. Generous in his gifts, overflowing with liberality, a refuge for the orphan and the destination of all who came seeking.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: distance, separation
one who bows his head in respect, reverential
cheerful, jovial, pleasant
fragrant of embrace, sweet-smelling
slender, slim-waisted
a powerful, massive lion
with rough, thick paws (said of a lion)
the gift, bounty; generous giving

context: This elegy praises Sakhr ibn Amr, Al-Khansa's brother, addressing him directly as a man of noble character who was both approachable with family and strangers, and fierce as a lion in battle — a shelter for orphans and a fountain of generosity.

titleEn: I lay awake while my companions slept
translationEn: I lay awake while my companions slept away from my vigil, as if fire blazed through my garments. Whenever a star set, it left me laden with eternal griefs that never return to their source. Abu Awfa has left behind qualities within me, and all of them have entered every valley of my being.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I lay awake, suffered from sleeplessness
sleeplessness, night-vigil
sets, sinks (said of a star)
eternal, everlasting (griefs)
a place of return, a refuge
virtues, noble qualities, traits

context: A short but deeply felt elegy in which Al-Khansa describes her sleepless grief for her brother Abu Awfa, saying the pain burns like fire in her clothes and every setting star brings fresh, endless sorrow.

titleAr: ما بال عينيك منها دمعها سرب
titleEn: What ails your eyes that their tears flow

verses.sadr: ما بال عينيك منها دمعها سرب
أم ذكر صخر بعيد النوم هيجها
يا لهف نفسي على صخر إذا ركبت
قد كان حصنا شديد الركن ممتنعا
verses.ajuz: ليثا إذا نزل الفتيان أو ركبوا
صاف ، عتيق ، فما في وجهه ندب

verses.sadr: يا فارس الخيل إذ شدت رحائلها
كم من ضرائك هلاك و ارملة
سقيا لقبرك من قبر ولا برحت
ماذا تضمن من جود و من كرم
verses.ajuz: و من خلائق ما فيهن مقتضب

translationEn: What ails your eyes that their tears flow so freely — was it grief that startled them, or is joy returning? Or is it the memory of Sakhr that disturbed their sleep, so that tears pour from them over him forever? How I grieve for Sakhr when horses race against horses, calling to each other and charging in confusion! He was a fortress strong in its corner, impregnable — a lion whether the young men were mounted or afoot. Bright, luminous, like the full moon in his countenance, pure and noble, with no scar upon his face. O rider of horses when their saddles were tightened, and feeder of the starving poor when hunger struck! How many of the destitute, the bereaved, and widows settled at your door and their sorrows lifted. May your grave be watered well — may the pouring rains not cease to drench it and fill it. What generosity, what nobility, what character does your grave contain — qualities without flaw.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: flowing freely, streaming
far from sleep, sleepless
pours down, flows
bright-faced, noble, illustrious
luminous, radiant, bright
scar, blemish
they were hungry, suffered from hunger
thunderclouds bringing rain

context: Al-Khansa mourns Sakhr by questioning her own eyes about their constant weeping, then praising him as a stronghold for his tribe, a lion in war, and a feeder of the hungry — ending with a prayer that rain may forever water his grave.

titleAr: يا عين جودي بدمع منك مسكوب
titleEn: O eye, pour forth your tears in streams

verses.sadr: يا عين جودي بدمع منك مسكوب
نعم الفتى كان للأضياف إذ نزلوا
كم من مناد دعا و الليل مكتنع
و من اسير بلا شكر جزاك به

translationEn: O eye, pour forth your tears, poured out like pearls strung and pierced on a necklace. I remembered him while the night was thick with darkness, and in my heart is a crack that cannot be mended. What a fine young man he was for guests when they alighted, and a helper to one awakened from sleep to find himself plundered. How many a caller cried out in the dark of night, and he loosened from him the ropes of death while the man was distressed. And how many a prisoner, bearing wounds with no word of thanks, he freed, speaking only good words after releasing him — words that none could accuse of falsehood.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: poured out, flowing
necklace strings, rows of pearls
pierced, bored through
thick, dense (of darkness)
mended, joined (crack that is not mended)
dense, enveloping (of night)
healing over (wounds that have not healed)
to be accused, to have a charge leveled against

context: Al-Khansa calls upon her eyes to weep for Sakhr, recalling him as a rescuer of those crying out in the night, a ransomer of prisoners, and a man of generous and truthful speech who freed the captive and asked nothing in return.

titleAr: تقول نساء : شبت من غير كبرة ،
titleEn: Women say I have aged without growing old

verses.sadr: تقول نساء : شبت من غير كبرة ،
verses.ajuz: و ايسر مما قد لقيت يشيب

verses.sadr: أقول : أبا حسان : لا العيش طيب
verses.ajuz: و كيف و قد افردت منك يطيب

verses.sadr: فتى السن كهل الحلم لا متسرع
أخو الفضل لا باغ عليه لفضله
verses.ajuz: ولا هو خرق في الوجوه قطوب

verses.sadr: اذا ذكر الناس السماح من امرئ
لعمري لقد أوهيت قلبي عن العزا
verses.ajuz: و طأطأت رأسي و الفؤاد كئيب

translationEn: Women say I have aged without growing old — yet something far lesser than what I have endured turns hair white. I say: O Abu Hassan, life is no longer sweet, and how could it be sweet now that I am left alone without you? You were a youth in age but mature in wisdom — neither rash nor miserly with clenched fists. A man of virtue, none could find fault with his generosity, nor was he rough-faced and sullen in public. Whenever people mentioned liberality or when an orator said what was right and noble, I remembered you and tears flowed while my chest was tight with a lump that makes the heart dissolve. By my life, you have weakened my heart against consolation, you have bowed my head while my heart is sorrowful. You have broken in me a strong spear-shaft — and even the shaft of a nabaʿ tree breaks though it is hard.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you have aged, you have grown grey
old age, great age
I was left alone, separated
clenched-fisted, miserly

frowning, scowling
a hard, strong spear-shaft

context: Al-Khansa responds to women who note that grief has aged her prematurely, addressing her dead brother Sakhr (Abu Hassan) directly, cataloguing his virtues of wisdom, generosity, and composure, and lamenting that his loss has broken her strength like a shattered spear.

titleEn: O eyes, weep for Sakhr in full measure

verses.ajuz: اذا الخيل من طول الوجيف اقشعرت

verses.sadr: شددت عصاب الحرب اذ هي مانع
وكانت اذا ما رامها قبل حالب

verses.ajuz: إذا ما رحى الحرب العوان استدرت

translationEn: O eyes, weep for Sakhr in full measure when the horses shudder from the length of their swift trotting. When they are urged onward in the battle-cry and they fall into step like dogs barking and snarling at each other in a fight. He would tighten the bandage of war when it was difficult, and she would kick her legs, straining and letting down milk. She was one that, when a milker tried to approach her, she would defend herself with spurts of blood and contract. Abu Hassan Sakhr struck her and stirred her with the spear until she submitted. Endurance was your nature when the millstone of a long war would grind. They steadied both sides of her head and aided each other against her in the day of battle, and she stretched out flat. A battle-seasoned she-camel, biting, whose young is not called — impregnated with spear-shafts until she continued. You swore an oath that the banner-bearers would be struck down, and the horses did not make you break your oath until you destroyed them. And horses calling to each other with no truce between them — you drove past them to the herds and they passed on. He was like a proud lion from the lions of Tabala, ever present wherever they wheeled and charged.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: generously, abundantly, in full measure
swift trotting of horses

context: This complex battle-elegy uses the extended metaphor of a fierce war-camel to praise Sakhr's indomitable spirit in combat, describing how he alone could subdue what was untamable and how he honored his battle-oaths unto death.

titleAr: لهفي على صخر فاني ارى له
titleEn: My grief for Sakhr — I see his generous gifts

verses.sadr: لهفي على صخر فاني ارى له
ولهفي على صخر لقد كان عصمة

verses.ajuz: اذا ما الموالي من اخيها تخلت

verses.sadr: ومختنق راخى ابن عمر و خناقه

verses.ajuz: غداة غد من اهلها ما استقلت

verses.sadr: فتى كان ذا حلم أصيل وتؤدة
verses.ajuz: اذا ما الحبى من طائف الجهل حلت

verses.sadr: وما كر الا كان اول طاعن
فيدرك ثأرا ثم لم يخطه الغنى
verses.ajuz: فمثل أخي يوما به العين قرت

translationEn: My grief for Sakhr — for I see his acts of generosity and beneficence have now departed. And my grief for Sakhr, for he was truly a refuge for his client when his client's foot slipped. He would return to his client with compassion when fellow tribesmen abandoned their kinsman's sister. You were such that when a needy hand came to you, hoping for a gift from your cloud, it was drenched. And one who was strangled — Ibn Amr loosened his collar and lifted the darkness from his face so it cleared. And a woman preparing to depart the tribe — but for his gift on the morrow she would never have risen from her people. You were life and sustaining shade for us — whenever we wished, his generosity would begin to pour. A youth of genuine wisdom and deliberateness when the bonds of foolish anger would loosen. Whenever he charged he was the first to strike, and horses shuddered whenever they saw him. He would achieve vengeance and then prosperity would not escape him — such a brother was one whose sight brought joy to the eyes. If they sought blood-vengeance he would begin it with their own debts, and he would endure protecting them when the horses fled. I do not remember after him a great misfortune — I recall him and the grief abates and clears.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my grief, my lamentation, my longing
acts of generosity, extra gifts, bounties
a refuge, a protection, a safeguard

destitute, lacking in means
one who is strangled, one in great distress
deliberateness, calm composure, steadiness
blood-vengeance, a debt of blood

context: A long and powerful elegy in which Al-Khansa recounts how Sakhr was a lifeline for the poor, the oppressed, and the threatened, before describing his prowess in battle and concluding that remembering him somehow lifts her grief — a paradox of mourning.

titleAr: ألا يا عين فانهمري ، وقلت ألا يا عين فانهمري ، وقلت
titleEn: O eye, pour down — I said, O eye pour

verses.sadr: ألا يا عين فانهمري ، وقلت ألا يا عين فانهمري ، وقلت
لو أن الكف تقبل في فداه

translationEn: O eye, pour down — for I said, O eye, pour down — over a calamity that struck me and then passed on. A calamity such that the soul, kept from sleep by it, blazes on the day it flares. O eye, woe to you — help me! For his calamity is great and has become immense. His calamity has fallen upon me and terrified me — it is both particular and universal in its reach. If the hand could be accepted as ransom for him, I would have offered my right hand until it withered. Just as he lavished upon us continuously from his generosity and built noble deeds for us until they poured down. He never desisted and his hands never fell short, yet my praise of him has not reached to where he now dwells.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pour down, flow (imperative to the eye)
a great calamity, a devastating loss
boiled over, flared up (of intense grief)
woe to you (expression of reproach or sorrow)
withered, became paralyzed (of a hand)
continued repeatedly, lavished continuously
built up, raised (noble deeds)
my praise, my commendation

context: In this elegy Al-Khansa addresses her eye repeatedly, calling it to pour tears over an unnamed but enormous loss — likely Sakhr — saying the calamity is both personal and universal, and that her praise could never reach wherever he now rests.

titleEn: O eye, be generous with tears
translationEn: O eye, be generous with tears — the gushing, flowing kind — pouring like the overflow of brimming buckets from waterwheels. Weep for Sakhr, now that he lies between the tomb-slabs and the stone covering. A grave beside a burial mound strewn by wild-blowing winds. The chieftain, the magnanimous noble, and son of high-nosed, magnanimous chieftains. The one who bore the heavy, serious burdens from crushing calamities. The healer of broken bones, from those who are harmed and those who grant favors. The giver of a hundred white camels from among the noble, swift racing kind. The one who forgave great offenses for kinsmen and those with whom he shared salt. Doing so deliberately and with forbearance, when forbearance was deemed best. That was the one through whom we healed the sick griefs within our hearts. He would repel the enemy's sudden advance and the arrogance of the hostile antagonist. But the treachery of fate struck us and reached us with its horns charging. As if time had slaughtered our throats with the blades of sacrificial knives. Our women lament and wail after the lead mourner begins her dirge. They moan after the heavy sleep of eyes — the moaning of a bereaved, distracted woman. Disheveled, pale, worn out — they do not slacken even when the night of mourning tires. They lament the loss of my brother of generosity, goodness, and righteous character. And generosity, and long hands flowing with liberality and pardon. Now we and those beyond us are like the teeth of an aging camel — worn down to equals.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: gushing, pouring forth (of tears)
flowing, streaming (tears)
the tomb, the grave-slab
the magnanimous noble, the generous chieftain
crushing calamities, overwhelming disasters
white camels, noble camels
those with whom one has shared salt — friends, allies
horses or camels in their fifth year — aged animals

context: One of Al-Khansa's most celebrated long elegies for Sakhr, structured as a cascade of epithets praising him as chieftain, healer of the injured, forgiver, giver of camels, and protector — before describing the tribe's collective mourning and sense of leveling loss.

titleAr: ذري عنك أقوال الضلال ، كفى بنا
titleEn: Abandon the words of the misguided, we suffice

verses.sadr: ذري عنك أقوال الضلال ، كفى بنا
verses.ajuz: لكبش الوغى في اليوم و الأمس ناطحا
غداة علا نهجا من الحق واضحا

verses.sadr: فإن تك قد أبكتك سلمى بمالك

translationEn: Abandon the words of the misguided — it suffices that we have been the butting ram of battle, yesterday and today. For Khalid has more right to be excused than you, on the morning he took the clear path of righteousness. Against you, by the permission of God, he is steadfastly determined — omens of the right side and left side do not make him stumble. They announced the death of Malik crowned when we descended upon them, grim-faced in the billowing dust, with scowling looks. If Salma has made you weep over Malik, we have left upon him wailing women and wailing men.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: abandon, set aside, leave
the ram of battle — the foremost warrior
butting, charging with the head
right-side omens (favorable)
left-side omens (usually unfavorable)
grim-faced, fierce-looking
billowing, rising dust
with snarling faces, scowling

context: This is a poem of tribal pride and defiance, not a personal elegy — Al-Khansa boasts of her clan's martial prowess and their victories over enemies, asserting their right to lead and their fearlessness in battle.

titleEn: Do not think I have found any rest

verses.sadr: لا تخلني اني نسيت ولا بل

verses.ajuz: يردع الجهل بعد ما قد اشاحا

verses.sadr: انني قد علمت وجدك بالحمد م
كم طريد قد سكن الجأش منه

translationEn: Do not think that I have found any rest after Sakhr — not until mourning cries settle back in me. From my innermost being, the burn of grief is such that sorrow has torn open my heart like an old wound. Do not think I have forgotten — no, my heart, even if I drank pure water, remembers Sakhr whenever I recall his generosity; my patience with his loss is exhausted, and the grief has spoken out. Within my chest are four birds calling to each other in longing until they have broken the wing. My bones are crushed and my wing is broken within me — Sakhr has perished and I cannot bear to rest. Who now will receive the guest arriving at the tribe in distress after Sakhr, when he calls out for help? Upon him the tribe's widows and travelers rely, and those struck by hardship have been made apparent through him. And gifts he shakes out with liberality, and ambition for those who desired ambition. He seized all affairs, steadfast and noble, and whenever he aspired to battle he laid it open. With forbearance when the ignorant assailed him — he would repel their foolishness after they had turned away. I knew your passionate love of praise and your freeing of captives with generosity. A horseman who strikes the battalion with the sword when wailing and crying crowd together. He faces the stabbing at the throats with a sideways look, when he rises until wounds no longer matter. They advance against him until they turn away from him, retreating — and they do not seek him out face to face. How many a fugitive whose heart was calmed by him — he used to call to them openly by their ranks. Horseman of war and its turbaned leader, the champion of battle when it meets him head-on.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rest, ease, relief

the champion, the foremost defender

context: A long, passionate elegy in which Al-Khansa insists she has found no rest since Sakhr's death, cataloguing his role as protector of guests, widows, captives, and warriors, and praising his wisdom, generosity, and fearlessness in battle.

titleAr: جرى لي طير في حمام حذرته
titleEn: An omen bird flew among doves, I warned you

verses.sadr: جرى لي طير في حمام حذرته
verses.ajuz: عليك ابن عمر و من سنيح وبارح

verses.sadr: فلم ينج صخرا ما حذرت وغاله
فيا عين بكي لأمرىء طار ذكره
أخو الحزم في الهيجاء والعزم في التي

translationEn: An omen bird flew among the doves and I warned you of it, O son of Amr — both the right-side and left-side omen. But what I warned against could not save Sakhr, and the repeated visitations of death, morning and evening, destroyed him. He is now a pledge to the grave, its trailing edges of drifting sand blown over it by passing winds. O eye, weep for a man whose memory has taken flight — for him weep the eye of swift racing horses. And every long-shafted, dark, slender spear, and every noble sword among the finest blades. And every polished coat of mail like a gleaming pool, made to flow, and every swift charger among the best in its prime. And every steady trotting camel like a powerful bull-camel in its shaggy covering, and every swift rider in the last hours of night, pressing on. And the neighbor's eye weeps for him whenever he called on a day when a host was needed, calling for help first by his right-side auspices. He was the man of resolve in battle and determination in what, when it fell, blackened the bright polished armor. Of noble lineage, keen of mind, spending freely what he gained, bestowing the inherited wealth of the deceitful hostile enemy.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a right-side omen (favorable bird passing from left to right)

context: Al-Khansa recalls seeing an ill omen before Sakhr's death and warns that it could not be averted, then eulogizes him through a long series of images: every weapon, horse, camel, and warrior that weeps for him, before praising his nobility and generosity.

titleEn: O my two eyes, be generous and do not freeze
translationEn: O my two eyes, be generous and do not freeze! Will you not weep for Sakhr the generous? Will you not weep for the bold, the beautiful, the noble youth and chieftain? Long in his sword-belt, lofty in his glory, he led his tribe as a beardless youth. When people stretched forth their hands toward glory, he stretched his toward it too. And he attained what was beyond their reach in glory, then continued ascending. People burdened him with whatever burdened them, even though he was the youngest born among them. You could see glory rushing to his house, for he considered being praised the finest thing to earn. And whenever glory was mentioned you would find him girded with glory, then wrapped in it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: freeze, dry up, become still (of tears)
generosity, liberality, dew
long in the sword-belt — tall, of great stature
high in the tent-pole — of great honor and distinction
as a beardless youth, in early youth
rushes toward, plunges down to

put on an upper garment, draped himself

context: A celebrated elegy for Sakhr in which Al-Khansa praises him for achieving leadership even as a young man, surpassing his elders in the pursuit of glory, and making his house the destination of honor itself — imagery that became iconic in Arabic elegiac poetry.

titleEn: My eye wept and sleeplessness returned

verses.sadr: فكم من فارس لك أم عمر و
كصخر او معاوية بن عمر و

translationEn: My eye wept and sleeplessness returned to me, and I passed the night leaning, grief-stricken. From the memory of a people who departed and left behind them their succession as a loss upon us. They reached the fifth-day thirst and by evening they had joined the departed, following in the steps of Thamud. How many a horseman has the mother of Amr, whose spear-tip protects the isolated weak person — like Sakhr or Muawiya ibn Amr, when the faces of men turn dark with fear. He drives back the horses with their sides bleeding — worthy on a day of battle to take captives. They would slaughter their prized she-camels for those who came to them, when a hundred head was not considered a great gift.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sleeplessness, insomnia
leaning, bending to one side (from grief)
grief-stricken, propped up (by sorrow)
the fifth-day thirst (interval between waterings for camels)
the isolated, the lonely, the one separated from his group
both sides (of the horse's body)
she-camels in their tenth month of pregnancy — most prized
the newborn, a young child

context: Al-Khansa mourns both her brothers Sakhr and Muawiya together in this poem, lamenting that their generation has followed the ancient destroyed people of Thamud, and praising both brothers as fearless warriors and open-handed hosts.

titleAr: لا شيء يبقى غير وجه مليكنا
titleEn: Nothing endures save the face of our King

verses.sadr: لا شيء يبقى غير وجه مليكنا
verses.ajuz: ولست أرى شيئا على الدهر خالدا

verses.sadr: ألا إن يوم ابن الشريد ورهطه

verses.ajuz: ومن كان من عليا هوازن شاهدا

verses.sadr: بأن بني ذبيان قد أرصدوا لكم
verses.ajuz: إذا ما تلاقيتم بأن لا تعاودا

verses.sadr: فقد زاح عنا اللوم اذ تركوا لنا
فقد جرت العادات أنا لدى الوغى

translationEn: Nothing endures but the face of our King, and I see nothing that remains eternal through time. The day of Ibn al-Sharid and his people has wiped out our cauldrons and the settled cooking pots. They used to fill the orphan's bowl and they used to fulfil their promises to the friend. Convey from me to Sulaym and Amir, and to whoever of the nobles of Hawazin is present: that the sons of Dhubyan have prepared for you, when you meet, to receive no further reprieve. Let none approach the land except a stealthy thief who fears an army at sunrise, eager and withholding. Upon every smooth-coated, lean she-camel, in the last of the night, not yet made to kneel while bearing iron loads. Blame has been lifted from us since they left for us the heights, the rising ground, and the watering-place with its visitors. We have slain Hashim and his nephew, and there shall be no peace until we exact vengeance for our captive women. Custom has established that when we go to war we shall be victorious, and every person seeks his advantage.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: our King — referring to God
enduring forever, eternal
stationary, settled (of large cooking pots)
Dhubyan — a tribe hostile to Al-Khansa's clan
prepared, lay in wait
smooth-coated, with little shedding hair — sleek camel
captive women, young women taken prisoner
advantages, benefits, gains

context: Opening with a meditation on the transience of all things except God, this poem pivots to a tribal war poem in which Al-Khansa conveys a warning to enemy tribes and asserts her clan's determination to fight on until blood-vengeance is taken.

titleEn: I weep for Sakhr when a ringed dove moans

verses.ajuz: ومارن العود لا كز ولا عاد

verses.sadr: سمح الخليقة لا نكس ولا غمر
verses.ajuz: بل باسل مثل ليث الغابة العادي

verses.sadr: من أسد بيشة يحمي الخل ذي لبد

translationEn: I weep for Sakhr whenever a ringed dove moans in her grief, a grey dove in the valley. When warriors are clad in interlocking coats of mail, and polished blades white as salt glitter in rows. And a nabaʿ bow that twangs and wails, with a flexible shaft that is neither stiff nor deviant. He was generous of character, neither weak nor ignorant — rather, bold like the predatory lion of the forest. From the lions of Bisha, protecting the friend with the thick mane against his settled and wandering folk. And the one who fed his people fat when they were hungry in a sideways dusty wind that blew with bitter cold.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a ringed or collared dove
her grief, her sorrow
grey, dust-colored (a dove)
coats of mail, armor
doubled, interlocking (of chain mail)
a sharp, cutting sword
a bow made from the nabaʿ tree
bitter cold (with a northerly wind)

context: This elegy begins with the image of a mourning dove whose cry triggers Al-Khansa's tears for Sakhr, before she praises him through battle imagery — his prowess as a lion, his generosity in providing meat in cold and hungry times.

titleEn: O eye, be generous with tears, for your springs have dried
translationEn: O eye, be generous with tears, for your springs have dried up. And weep for Sakhr — he has torn open the heart with what it endures. He was the one sought as a host in years when the arid lands grew hard. When the winds blow at night, driving their fierce gusts with bitter cold. Stripping from the sky's surface the wisps of cloud while the water is frozen solid. Torn shreds that the wind chases as if they were fugitive rags in flight. And wealth with those who have reserves, and riches are packs of fleeing animals. He would release the distress of one whose she-camels have been sucked dry of their marrow — pure of the bucket-ropes, hard-tried. Until he returns with what he returns with — abundant kindness, one who is praised. Your generosity was ever-present and your light blazed in the thick darkness. If the thirsty camels were set free to fatten with no guide, they would head straight for you, led by your bounty and the well-worn paths. People pour toward you in streams — some departing with wealth and some arriving. They come to you like a surging sea in which thunderclouds have rained their downpours. O son of the wise, intelligent nobles, and son of the great generous supporters! And son of the like marrying like — adorned with the noblest character. A protector of those who call when flight-panic seizes a man at death. And strong hands for those who perish — long-standing leaders of assembled armies.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: springs, sources (of tears)
arid, barren lands that give no pasture
bitterly cold, frozen
the surface of the sky, the sky's smooth expanse
the generous, the abundantly giving
supporters, those who aid and sustain others
a roaring surge, a tumultuous flood
assembled armies, gathered masses of men

context: A long and richly imagined elegy in which Al-Khansa praises Sakhr as the ultimate host in seasons of winter hardship and drought, his generosity likened to a guiding light in darkness and a sea to which all thirsty camels would instinctively come.

titleAr: أهاج لك الدموع على ابن عمر و
titleEn: What has stirred your tears over Ibn Amr

verses.sadr: أهاج لك الدموع على ابن عمر و
فإن تك قد أتتك فلا تنادي

translationEn: What has stirred your tears over Ibn Amr — calamities that have struck you — so pour them out! With a great bucket flowing down over him, never ceasing like beads strung on a necklace. Over a noble branch — Khansa has been bereaved of him — of long reach and overflowing generosity, praised. Steadfast, he was the best of the sons of Sulaym — their noble one, both the one who leads and the one led. Abu Hassan was the support of my people, and now he lies settled among the tomb-slabs. A pledge to decay — and every youth will decay — so let your tears flow in generous pouring. I swear, had you lived you would have been among us a force that no multitude could outnumber. But calamities come knocking, turning upon the steadfast man. If fate has come for you, there is no calling out — for it has destroyed a generous, glorious man. Steadfast, determined, calamities of time have come upon him long ago, after the sons of Thamud. And Aad, upon whom time forced itself, and Himyar and armies upon armies. May Abu Hassan Sakhr not be far away, and may the birds of good fortune settle upon his grave.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: stirred up, provoked (tears or emotions)
a large bucket, a great outpouring
a single bead — lone pearl on a string
overflowing with generosity
the support, the refuge, the sustainer of a people
the niches of graves, tomb-slabs
the reversals of time, the blows of fate
birds of good fortune, lucky omens

context: Al-Khansa mourns Sakhr (Abu Hassan) as the indispensable pillar of her people, comparing the loss of him to the destruction of ancient peoples like Thamud and Aad — fates that even the strongest cannot escape — and ending with a prayer for his grave.

titleEn: O my two eyes, pour generously your tears

verses.ajuz: جودا ولا تعدا في اليوم موعودا

verses.sadr: هل تدريان على من ذا سبلتكما
verses.ajuz: على ابن امي ابيت الليل معمودا

verses.sadr: دارت بنا الارض او كادت تدور بنا
verses.ajuz: يا لهف نفسي فقد لاقيت صنديدا

verses.sadr: يا عين فابكي فتى محضا ضرائبه

verses.ajuz: ولا تراه اذا ما قام محدودا

verses.sadr: ولا يقوم إلى ابن العم يشتمه
قد عشت فينا ولا ترمى بفاحشة

translationEn: O my two eyes, pour generously from your tears — pour and do not delay the promised gift today. Do you know over whom you have let them flow? Over the son of my mother whom I spend the night leaning, propped up by grief. The earth has spun around us or nearly spun — O how I grieve my soul, for I have lost a formidable man. O eye, weep for a youth whose qualities were pure, difficult to watch over but easy when approached. He did not accept humiliation among a people and make them angry, nor could you see him standing before them broken and limited. He would not rise against his cousin to insult him, nor would he creep toward the neighbor-women in deceit. As if the Compassionate God created his face like a pure gold dinar that people see as tested and fine. Go, you who has been bereaved — may God reward you with His paradise from us, and may you dwell in the Garden eternally. You lived among us and were never accused of a shameful act, until the Lord of all people took you while you were praised.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: let them flow, released them (of tears)
propped up, leaning (from grief)
a formidable, mighty man
pure in his qualities, unmixed in character
humiliation, degradation
limited, restricted, bounded (broken)
sneaking, creeping with bad intentions
one stripped of his possessions, bereaved

context: An elegy addressed to her own eyes, Al-Khansa mourns the son of her mother — likely Sakhr — praising his moral integrity, handsome appearance compared to a gold dinar, and his untarnished reputation, before praying for his entry into Paradise.

titleEn: The earth has grown narrow for me, its sacred bounds broken

verses.sadr: يا صخر قد كنت بدرا يستضاء به
verses.ajuz: فقد ثوى يوم مت المجد والجود

verses.sadr: فاليوم امسيت لا يرجوك ذو امل

translationEn: The earth has grown narrow for me and its sacred limits have been shattered, until the landmarks and the open plains have become humble and subdued. Those who say 'console yourself from remembering him — patience cannot reject the decree of God' speak truly. O Sakhr, you were a full moon whose light we used to follow; on the day you died, glory and generosity died with you. Today no one who hopes still hopes for you, now that you have perished — and the pool of death is one that all must reach. How many a terrifying frontier did you plunge into its depths on your close-bred horses with brave young men upon them. You set before the people in it the decision of their eyes like a shooting star, and they scattered from you in all directions.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its sacred and forbidden bounds, its inviolable limits
became humble, bowed down (of landmarks)
prominent landmarks, mountain peaks
the open desert plains
console yourself, seek consolation
close-bred horses, prized horses kept near the tent
a shooting star, a meteor
scattered in all directions, dispersed

context: Al-Khansa describes the world itself as narrowing and collapsing with Sakhr's death, then addresses him directly as a full moon whose light guided the people — gone now — before praising his fearlessness at the frontiers of battle.

titleAr: يا ابن الشريد وخير قيس كلها
titleEn: O son of al-Sharid, best of all Qays

verses.sadr: يا ابن الشريد وخير قيس كلها
انت المهند من سليم في العلى

translationEn: O son of al-Sharid, best of all Qays — you have left me in grief and confusion. I will weep for you as long as I hear a dove calling her cooing in the branches of the Farkad stars. You are the Indian sword of Sulaym in matters of glory, the noble branch that has never shamed the noble in any gathering. You were a fortress for the entire tribe and its spokesman before the mighty, the high-born. So go, and may you not be far — every person who lives long will taste the bitter cup of death. God bless the sons of Nuhasr — they demolished the tent-pole and seized it with their black swords. Of great generosity, his lineage is noble, like the full moon or a rising like the star of good fortune.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: confusion, stupefaction, heaviness of grief
the cooing of a dove, a dove's call
a star in Ursa Minor, used as a navigation point
the Indian sword — finest quality blade
the mighty, high-born, noble chieftain
the central tent-pole — metaphor for the tribal pillar
with black swords, with the lion (also 'aswad')
the most fortunate star; also a proper name meaning 'most happy'

context: Al-Khansa addresses Sakhr as the finest man of the entire Qays confederation, vowing to weep for him as long as doves call — a typical motif of eternal mourning — and praising him as her tribe's fortress and spokesperson before kings.

titleEn: I weep for my father Amr with a flowing eye

verses.sadr: وصخرا ومن ذا مثل صخر اذا غدا

translationEn: I weep for my father Amr with an eye that flows abundantly — little does it sleep even when the carefree sleep their fill. And for my two brothers — I do not forget Muawiya, who received the delegations from the nobles of the two lava-plains. And Sakhr — and who is like Sakhr when in his courtyard the tall, gaunt men come leading their flocks? That is the true calamity, O Hind, so know it — and the fires of war blazing when their fuel was lit.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: abundant, copious, flowing freely
its sleep (of the eye)
my two brothers (dual of 'brother' or 'peer')
nobles, chieftains, leaders
the two lava-plains — a geographic reference in Arabia
the tall, lean men; the thin-flanked
short or impoverished people; thin, lean
the true calamity, the devastating loss

context: A short, condensed elegy in which Al-Khansa mourns three men at once: her father Amr and her two brothers Muawiya and Sakhr, addressing the poem to a woman named Hind and calling these losses the true definition of calamity.

titleEn: O eye, pour down with treachery's grief

verses.sadr: اذا ما الضيق حل الى ذراه

translationEn: O eye, pour down — pour with treachery's overflow — and flow a flowing without stint. Do not seek consolation after Sakhr, for consolation has been overwhelmed and my patience exhausted. Over a calamity that left the innermost chest sleepless, feeling the heat of live coals. Over Sakhr — and what youth is like Sakhr for a debtor who is poor and bound by blood-vengeance? And for the hardened adversary when he transgressed, to take the right of the oppressed by force. And for guests when they arrived by night, and for the burdened poor and every traveler. When a year of frozen hardship settled upon them, she-camels whose milk never dried up with churning mud. There he was a rain, a life-giving downpour — his generosity found in a courtyard free of filth. More life-giving than a sheltered young woman, and braver than the father of a lion-cub. Wide-mouthed and tawny, a savage lion that, when it charged, its charge could not be halted by shouting. A massive lion using his own forearms as a pillow on the raiding paths and every sea. The she-lions in their dens would submit to him whenever they heard his roaring at every dawn. Solid and immovable — no visitor comes to her for hardship or ease of time. Now he lies settled in a grave in a barren battlefield of souls. The riders who came from him hard-pressed still mount from him a bold, noble, profound man. Whenever the burdened came to his protection he received them with an unclouded face. He opened every door through his generosity — hiding himself behind no curtain from them. Calamities struck me through him, and now their anxieties come upon me morning and evening. If time were ever to choose a companion, its companion would have been Sakhr ibn Amr.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: in treachery, with the treachery of fate
scanty, meager, insufficient
consolation, condolence
a frozen, barren year of drought
she-lions in their dens, lionesses in hiding
a powerful, massive lion
sullen, frowning, displeased of face
conceals himself, hides behind a screen

context: One of the most expansive elegies for Sakhr, culminating in the famous final couplet that time itself would have chosen Sakhr as its companion had it taken one. Al-Khansa praises his role as a lion in battle and as the most open-handed host, whose door had no curtain to hide him from those in need.

titleEn: Is there a mote in your eye or is it smarting

verses.ajuz: ام ذرفت اذخلت من اهلها الدار

verses.sadr: تبكي لصخر هي العبرى وقد ولهت
تبكي خناس فما تنفك ما عمرت
تبكي خناس على صخر وحق لها
verses.ajuz: اذ رابها الدهر ان الدهر ضرار

verses.sadr: لا بد من ميتة في صرفها عبر
قد كان فيكم أبو عمر و يسودكم
يا صخر وراد ماء قد تناذره
verses.ajuz: أهل الموارد ما في ورده عار

verses.sadr: وما عجول على بو تطيف به
ترتع ما رتعت ، حتى إذا ادكرت
لا تسمن الدهر في ارض وان رتعت
فقلت لما رأيت الدهر ليس له
لقد نعى ابن نهيك لي اخا ثقة

verses.ajuz: حتى أتى دون غور النجم أستار

verses.sadr: ولا تراه وما في البيت يأكله
قد كان خالصتي من كل ذي نسب
في جوف لحد مقيم قد تضمنه
طلق اليدين لفعل الخير ذو فجر
لا يمنع القوم إن سالوه خلعته

translationEn: Is there a mote in your eye, or is it smarting, or did it weep because the people departed from their abode? As if my eye at his memory, when it passes through my mind, is a flood running ceaselessly over my cheeks. Weeping for Sakhr — she is grief-stricken and has gone mad, and between her and him are the curtains of fresh earth. Khansa weeps and will not cease as long as she lives — she has a keening for him and she is bereft. Khansa weeps for Sakhr and it is her right, for time has wronged her — and time is ever a wrongdoer. Death is inescapable in its turnings, and time in its turns has phases and cycles. Abu Amr was among you, your chieftain — what a fine turbaned one for those who called, a helper. Hard of backbone, generous when others refused, and in wars bold of breast, crushing. O Sakhr, one who came to a water-hole that people warned each other about — there was no shame in his coming to it. He walked with the lion's stride to a battle that was difficult, with two weapons: fangs and claws. Like a she-camel circling around her calf — she has two kinds of longing: open and secret. She grazes as long as she grazes, but when she remembers, she is only coming and going. She will never grow fat all her life in any land though she grazes, for she is only pining and restless. No day shall I feel his loss more than the day Sakhr parted from me — and time has its sweets and its bitters. Sakhr is our master and chieftain, and Sakhr in winter is our slaughterer of camels. Sakhr is the first to charge when they ride, and Sakhr when they are hungry is the crusher of grain. Sakhr is the beacon the guides follow, as if he were a landmark with fire upon his head. Tough, handsome of face, complete and pious — and in wars at the morning of terror, a kindler of fire. Bearer of banners, descender into valleys, witness at councils, at the head of great armies. I said when I saw that time has no one to reproach — it alone weaves warp and weft: Ibn Nuhayk announced to me a trusted brother whose news was foretold before the announcement came. I spent the night sleepless, watching the star until the curtains came before the setting star. No neighbor-woman ever saw him walking in her courtyard with ill intent when the neighbor left his house empty. You would not see him eating what was in the house — rather he was out in the open courtyard, generous and noble. He who fed the people fat in their hunger and in drought was noble of grandfather, prosperous. He was my purest kinsman of all those of lineage — he has been struck down and life has no longer any delight. Like the spear of Rudayna, whose youth did not run out, as if under the folds of the cloak he wore were iron bracelets. Grim-faced yet his countenance lights up the night — his fathers were tall of frame and free. Inheritor of glory, blessed in his inner nature, great in his gifts, a warrior in times of hardship. Noble branch of a noble branch, pure and unmixed — firm of resolve before assemblies, a man of pride. Lying in the hollow of a grave, settled, contained in his tomb with its bundled shrouds and stones. Open-handed for doing good, a man of generosity — great in his gifts, a guide to all things good. Let the destitute man weep for him, whose property time has destroyed and whose companion is misery and poverty. And a group whose guide got lost in a wasteland as if its darkness in the cloudy night were pitch. He would not refuse the people if they asked for his outer garment, nor would a passer-by in the night go past him without shelter.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a mote, a speck in the eye
smarting, inflammation of the eye
flowing ceaselessly, copious (of rain or tears)
bereft, dazed with grief
a lion walking with a proud, rolling gait

a spear attributed to Rudayna, renowned for its quality
a warrior who raids frequently, a swift raider

context: The most celebrated and longest elegy of Al-Khansa's diwan, this poem opens with the iconic image of the grief-stricken eye and builds through a famous anaphoric section 'Sakhr is our chieftain... Sakhr in winter... Sakhr when they ride...' before cataloguing his virtues as a brave warrior, generous host, and pure-lineaged noble.

titleEn: O my two eyes, will you not weep for Sakhr

verses.ajuz: بدمع حثيث لا بكيء ولا نزر
على ذي الندى والجود والسيد الغمر

verses.sadr: فما لكما عن ذي يمينين فابكيا
verses.ajuz: عليه مع الباكي المسلب من صبر

verses.sadr: كأن لم يقل أهلا لطالب حاجة
ولم يغد في خيل مجنبة القنا

verses.ajuz: لتغدو على الفتيان بعدك أو تسري

verses.sadr: فمن يضمن المعروف في صلب ماله
verses.ajuz: ضمانك أو يقري الضيوف كما تقري

verses.sadr: جراد زفته ريح نجد إلى البحر
verses.ajuz: وكائن قرنت الحق من ثوب صفوة

verses.sadr: ومن سابح طرف ومن كاعب بكر
لتدركه : يا لهف نفسي على صخر
verses.ajuz: ألا ثكلت أم الذين مشوا به

verses.sadr: إلى القبر ماذا يحملون إلى القبر
من الخير يا بؤس الحوادث والدهر
verses.ajuz: وم الحزم في العزاء والجود والندى
لقد كان في كل الأمور مهذبا
وان تلقه في الشرب لا تلقى فاحشا

translationEn: O my two eyes, will you not weep for Sakhr with quick tears — not scanty and not meager? And drain your tears or shed them over the one of generosity, liberality, and the flooding chieftain. What excuse do you have from one of two blessed hands — weep then over him together with the one who weeps, robbed of patience. As if he never said 'welcome' to one who sought something — though he was bright of face and open of chest. As if he never rode out in horses flanked by spears to water the tips of dark Rudaynite lances. But such is the way of fate — when its treachery struck you, it would go out upon the young men after you, morning and evening. Who can guarantee beneficence from his own deep wealth as you guaranteed it? Or receive guests as you received them? Like locusts driven by the Najdi wind to the sea. How many a good garment have you paired with truth — from a choice robe and a swimming swift horse and a full-breasted virgin. And a woman saying as the bier has outpaced her steps to reach it: O how my soul grieves for Sakhr! May the mothers of those who carried him to the grave be bereaved! What is it they carry to the grave? And what does the grave conceal beneath its earth of goodness? O the misery of calamities and time! And determination in the face of consolation, and generosity, and open-handedness — on the morning when the companion of ease and hardship is seen. He was refined in all matters, great in his acts of kindness — not to be restrained by a scolding. And if you encountered him at the drinking-session you would find no indecency. Nor one who breaks the bonds of secret compacts and endurance. May the grave that contains his person not be kept far from mercy — and may every dripping rain be generous over him.

context: In this elegy Al-Khansa challenges her own eyes to pour tears for Sakhr, then stages the scene of his funeral bier outpacing a mourning woman — a powerful dramatic image — before praising his refinement, open-handedness, and moral integrity, and concluding with a prayer for rain upon his grave.

titleAr: إن كنت عن وجدك لم تقصري
titleEn: If you have not fallen short in your grief

verses.sadr: إن كنت عن وجدك لم تقصري
verses.ajuz: او كنت في الأسوة لم تعذري

translationEn: If you have not fallen short in your grief, or if in your consolation you cannot be excused, then know that in the camp there is one who crosses the night journey on lean, young she-camels. A companion to whom I said, fearful: 'You are in full view of the horses.' You are one who calls out loudly — when you have reached the highest lookout, watch carefully. They spotted from a moment's time a horseman trotting at the nearest patch of the viewing place. He applied the whip to Hawshab — a sleek horse like a dusty-grey young male mountain-goat. Its leg works it with both striking together as the midday heat leans on the left man.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: passionate grief, intense love and sorrow
consolation, example to follow in bearing grief
young lean she-camels
in plain view, visible to lookouts
a lookout post, an elevated vantage point
trots, moves at a trot (of a horse)
sleek, smooth-coated (of a horse)
a dusty-grey young mountain ibex

context: A more narrative poem in which Al-Khansa describes a scout or companion keeping watch on an elevated lookout, spotting a rider in the distance and urging a fast horse forward — a vivid scene of tribal vigilance and readiness for war.

titleEn: I remembered my brother after the carefree had slept
translationEn: I remembered my brother after the carefree had slept, and tears poured from me in a rushing torrent. And horses that put on for their heroes a coat of mail and destroyed a people utterly. They hunt with the spear the leaders of the enemy and they seize the ram among them in a powerful grip. You drove them into the people beneath the battle and released your colt among them in a raid. He is certain, and you would think him returning — when they fell into step and covered the heat. Such is his dislike in earnestness, while in peace he plays and relaxes his lower garment. And a midday heat whose temperature is scorching — you made your cloak into a head-covering in it, to catch up with a pace near at hand and win praise and protect what is inviolable. You water the spear-tip and destroy the armored warrior like a cook's cauldron when it boils. You shade the horses over pools of spilled blood and give generously and slaughter the ten-month she-camels. When the saddle-frames were tied on a horse with markings, racing the bull-aurochs. Settled in the warmth of the roots of a tamarisk — did the evening rouse it and it burst forth? It wheeled, and when it saw the herd, it sensed prey nearby and flew. It ripped its outer garment in the noon-heat from the strain when the prey took flight at full speed. It spent the night hunting the heroes of the herd, and the sweat was wrung from it in torrents.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the carefree, the one without worry
flowing down, rushing downward
a coat of chain mail, armor
the foremost among them, the leaders and prime of the group
the ram — the leader, the champion
seizing and breaking (as a lion does to prey)
what is inviolable and must be protected, sacred honor
fresh dark blood, blood that has just been spilled

context: An elegy that unfolds as a vivid battle narrative, remembering a brother's heroism through scenes of mounted combat, the hunt of a lion among the wild herds, and feats of generosity in peacetime — the contrast of war and peace as twin expressions of noble character.

titleEn: The death-news arrived at Safina at dawn
translationEn: The death-news arrived at Safina at dawn, announcing the passing of the turbaned one from the sons of Amr. The protector of honor and the one who grants refuge whenever the sharp edge of time's calamities is feared. The tribe knows that his large bowl goes out in the morning of the wind or travels by night. And when it lit up and his cooking-pot boiled, what a fine master of the fire and the cauldron he was! Convey to his clients that they have been bereaved of a protector who feathered their arrows and did not sell them. He protected their defenders and gave them a hundred from the twenty-year-olds and the ten-year-olds. His lance-thrust waters the spear-tip while the horses have waded through running blood. He was the shelter of every widow and the lifter of the stumble of every man with an excuse. His acts of generosity reach their families, touching both the prosperous and the one in hardship.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: arrived at night, struck (of news or a visitor)
the announcement of death, the death-news
the one wearing the turban — the chieftain, the leader
a large wooden bowl used for food
his cooking pot, his cauldron
feathers their arrows — equips them, strengthens them
sells, betrays (he does not sell them out)
one who lifts another's stumble, who corrects mistakes

context: The final poem in this collection mourns a chieftain from the sons of Amr — likely Sakhr — whose death is announced at dawn to a place called Safina, praising him as the tribe's provider whose cooking-pot fed all in cold weather and whose generosity reached even the humblest widow and the man who had fallen short.

titleEn: O sons of Sulaym, if you meet Faqas
translationEn: O sons of Sulaym, if you meet the Faqas clan in a narrow pass leading to rough terrain, meet them with your swords and spears, and with a night raid like the pouring rain, until you scatter their assembly and remember Sakhr and how he was slain without vengeance being taken. And remember the horsemen of ours who were killed there in a stumble that fate dealt. He met Rabia in battle and was struck with a wound that pierced through to his chest — a straight spear with soft joints at its nodes, its blade keen as the eagle's leading talon. Rabia escaped that day in haste, running without slackening in his stride. A lean swift horse like an eagle swooping at dawn carried him. We took Khalid captive, and Awf granted him protection and released him in due measure. Our counsel intervened rightly in the matter of Khalid, something that would shame the horses to the end of time.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Faqas, a clan of the Asad tribe (enemies)
a narrow confining place, a tight pass
a night raid, a surprise attack
a wound piercing deep into the body cavity
flexible at the joints (said of a spear shaft)
sharp, keen (of a blade)
hard-pressed, pursued in haste
to slacken, to hold back

context: This poem is an incitement (tahrid) calling the Sulaym tribe to avenge the death of Sakhr, Al-Khansa's brother, who was killed without retribution. It also alludes to the capture and release of a man named Khalid, weaving tribal politics into the elegy's call for vengeance.

titleAr: يا عين فيضي بدمع منك مغزار
titleEn: O eye, pour forth abundant tears

verses.sadr: يا عين فيضي بدمع منك مغزار
وقد سمعت فلم أبهج به خبرا
قال ابن امك ثاو بالضريح وقد
فاذهب فلا يبعدنك الله من رجل
قد كنت تحمل قلبا غير مهتضم
لو منكم كان فينا لم ينل ابدا
و ابكوا فتى البأس وافته منيته
لا نوم حتى تقودوا الخيل عابسة

verses.ajuz: حلت على طبق من ظهرها عار
راموا الشكيمة من ذي لبدة ضار
ماض على الهول هاد غير محيار

translationEn: O my eye, pour forth tears in abundant floods, and weep for Sakhr with a pouring, ceaseless flow. I lay awake and spent the night in wakefulness, as though my eyes were rubbed with raw inflammation. I watch the stars without having taken on their tending, and at times I wrap myself in the excess of my garments. I heard a piece of news that brought me no joy — a messenger stood up spreading the tidings again and again: 'Your mother's son lies in his tomb, and they have covered him with planks and stones.' Depart then — may God not distance you — O man who repelled oppression and sought blood-vengeance. You bore a heart that was not crushed, set in a foundation that did not waver, like a spear-point whose form lights up the night, patient in hardship, a free man and son of free men. I weep for the young man of the tribe whom death has overtaken, for every soul has its appointed time and measure. I shall weep for you as long as the ring-dove coos and the night stars light the way for the traveller. I will make no peace with people who were your enemies until pitch turns white. Convey to the Sulaym and Awf, if you meet them, a message openly declared and not in secret: I mean those with whom he dwelled — do you know the sacred bond of guest and neighbour? Had he been among us, he would never have been harmed until matters of great consequence came to pass. He was as your cousin and your guest among you, yet you did not defend him — a breach of trust. Tighten your belts until the affair is settled for you, and gird yourselves, for these are days that demand girding. Weep for the warrior whose death came to him amid every calamity that befell and every decree. No sleep until you lead the horses grim-faced, casting down foals and mares in battle. Or dig a grave — for death lurks close by the houses of Husayn and Ibn Sayar. Or wash away the shame that has wrapped you, as menstrual cloths are washed clean at the time of purity. War has been ridden hard, like a humpbacked runaway camel, crouching upon a broad back left bare. It was as though all of them together, when they attempted him, attempted to seize the bit of a maned, deadly lion — guardian of his lair in battle, powerful, tearing men with fangs and claws — until the thousands scattered before a man who pressed forward through terror, a guide who never wavered, with a deep wound above his chest, bubbling with foaming dark blood from within.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: abundant, copious (of tears)
pouring constantly, incessant
lying still, dwelling (in the grave)
one who repels oppression / injustice
the black tar-box (proverbially black; 'until it turns white')
the sacred bond of protection owed to a guest or neighbour
broad-shouldered, powerful, capable
dark, flowing blood

context: One of Al-Khansa's longest and most powerful elegies for Sakhr, combining personal grief, sleepless nights, and a fierce call to the Sulaym tribe to avenge his death. It is notable for its sustained battle imagery, concluding with a vivid portrait of a lone warrior still standing though mortally wounded.

titleAr: عين فابكي لي على صخر إذا
titleEn: O eye, weep for me over Sakhr

verses.sadr: عين فابكي لي على صخر إذا

translationEn: O eye, weep for me over Sakhr whenever the blade rises above the haunches of the slaughtered camels, when the people are filled with fat whenever the wind bends the branches of the trees. And when the white women walk together like water-birds in the shallow, murky pool, leaning beneath the tips of the spears, their shins visible in a cautious mountain pass — he would deal a thrust that no healer's incantation could stanch, nor any bandaging of palm fibres.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the humps/backs of the slaughtered camels
bent, twisted (the wind bending branches)
water-birds (a simile for graceful women)
the shallow, turbid pool
leaning, inclining to one side
to stanch, to stop the flow (of blood)

context: A short, vivid elegy in which Al-Khansa links the memory of Sakhr to scenes of tribal generosity — the slaughter of camels to feed guests — and battle prowess, praising his lethal spear-thrust as something no remedy could heal.

titleAr: كأن ابن عمر و لم يصبح لغارة
titleEn: As though ibn Amr never rode to raid

verses.sadr: كأن ابن عمر و لم يصبح لغارة
فبكوا على صخر بن عمر و فإنه
verses.ajuz: يسير اذا ما الدهر بالناس اعسرا

translationEn: It is as though ibn Amr never set out at dawn for a raid with cavalry, nor drove lean, swift thoroughbred she-camels hard. As if he never crossed the land with true companions, shrouded in the dust that hooves kicked up into a dark cloud. As if he never built shade for his young men in the fierce midday heat — a decorated, embroidered mantle of shadow. Weep then for Sakhr ibn Amr, for he was easy-going when fate pressed hard upon people. He gave freely and was sweet when his goodness was sought, yet bitter as bitter colocynth when someone sought to provoke his bitterness. So al-Khansa weeps in the darkness, sorrowful, calling out to her brother who does not answer, lying face-down in the dust.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: noble, swift she-camels bred for riding
lean, spare (of camels trained for speed)
dust raised by hooves in battle or a raid
the fierce midday heats of summer
became difficult, pressed hard (of fate)
intensely bitter (like colocynth)
face-down in the dust, prostrated

context: A poignant elegy in which Al-Khansa imagines the world as if Sakhr had never existed — stripping away all his great deeds — before ending with the intimate image of herself, al-Khansa, weeping alone in the dark and calling out to her dead brother.

titleEn: O eye, pour forth your tears
translationEn: O my eye, be generous with tears for the noble, glorious youth — bright-faced and radiant as the sun, the finest of mankind. Even the sun is eclipsed at his perishing, and the moon is no longer full. Mankind weeps and wails for him, and the jinn cry out through the night. Even the wild beasts weep their grief when the news of him arrived. He was the great spokesman, the overflowing one, who bore the burdens of his clan upon his shoulders. He gave generously and never boasted of it, and hardheartedness was never his character. Woe upon woe is mine for him — I have become a fortress whose ramparts are broken.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the chief, the master (of men)
the glorious, bright-faced, illustrious
bright-faced, radiant, clear-browed
the chief spokesman, the foremost defender of his tribe
the overflowing one, the immensely generous
the abundant gift, lavish generosity
natural character, innate disposition

context: Al-Khansa mourns a great chieftain whose death is felt by all creation — humans, jinn, and wild beasts alike — a hyperbolic device common in pre-Islamic elegy, exalting the deceased's cosmic significance. The poem closes with the poetess herself as a broken fortress, her protector gone.

titleEn: Grief and sleeplessness visited me

verses.sadr: تبكي لصخر وقد راب الزمان به

verses.ajuz: وافي الذمام إذا ما معشر غدروا
عند المحول إذ ما هبت القرر

verses.sadr: ما بارز القرن يوما عند معركة
verses.ajuz: الا له يوم تسمو الكرة الظفر

translationEn: Sorrows and sleeplessness came and lodged with me; my eye flows in stillness with tears like pearls. She weeps for Sakhr, for time has turned suspicious against him — the accidents of days and fate consumed him. His character was generous, his gifts were ample, and he was true to his bond when others around him betrayed. He was the shelter of the destitute and the refuge of every widow in times of drought when the cold winds blew. Never did he face an opponent in battle without that day becoming for him a day when the tide of victory surged to triumph.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: came and settled with me (said of grief visiting one)
pearls (simile for beautiful, precious tears)
time became troubled/suspicious, turned against
the destitute, the one in need
years of drought, year of no rain
cold winds, the cold season
the counter-attack, the tide turning in battle

context: A short, intimate elegy cataloguing Sakhr's virtues as a protector of the weak — widows, the destitute — and as an invincible warrior, structured around the classic elegiac movement from grief to praise.

titleEn: My eyes, shed tears unstintingly

verses.sadr: يا صخر ! من لطراد الخيل إذ وزعت
ومن لكربة عان في الوثاق ، ومن
فر الاقارب عنها بعد ما ضربوا

verses.ajuz: من بعد لذة عيش غير مقتور

verses.sadr: يا صخر كنت لنا عيشا نعيش به
يا فارس الخيل إن شدوا فلم يهنوا
يا لهف نفسي على صخر إذا ركبت
يا صخر ماذا يواري القبر من كرم

translationEn: My two eyes, be generous with tears unstopped, and wail! For Sakhr is the best of those laid in graves. Do not fail me, for I shall never forget the memory of Sakhr, companion of glory and goodness. O Sakhr! Who now will drive the horses when they are spread for the chase, and tend the riding-camels when their saddles are strapped? Who is there for the orphans and the guests who arrive at our houses at night, known for deeds that were yours? And who for the anguish of a prisoner in bonds, who gave lavishly in hardship and in ease? Who now for the steady spear-thrust or the cry of a woman calling for help on the day of battle's clamour, when the raiders' horsemen ride? The near kinsmen fled after striking with the Mashrafi swords — a striking that was no warning blow. She was abandoned after the clashing of white blades, and had to make her hard way after the sweet life that was never stinted. O Sakhr, you were a life we lived by, had the calamities of fate only left you to us. O horseman of the cavalry when they charged without wavering, and horseman of the people when they intended to fall short. O how my soul aches for Sakhr, when horses charge against horses swift as oryx! He kindled war — a war that only firestarters, sons of firestarters, can kindle. O Sakhr, what does the grave conceal of nobility and characters pure and immaculate!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: stinted, held back, restrained
were spread out, distributed (horses in a chase)
the saddle (of a camel)
a prisoner, a captive
persistent, steady (said of a long thrust)
swords from the Mashrif region, renowned for quality
those who kindle fire; warriors who ignite war
pure, immaculate, morally clean

context: A long, searching elegy in which Al-Khansa piles up a series of rhetorical questions — 'who now will do what Sakhr did?' — to catalogue his indispensable roles as protector, warrior, and provider, before ending with the image of the grave hiding his boundless nobility.

titleAr: يا عين جودي بدمع غير منزور
titleEn: O eye, weep without restraint

verses.sadr: يا عين جودي بدمع غير منزور
نعم الفتى كنت إذ حنت مرفرفة

translationEn: O eye, be generous with tears unstopped, like pearls rolling down the cheeks. Weep for a brother whose qualities were praiseworthy, radiant like the crescent moon, not obscured. The horseman of the cavalry — fate came to him, and in my heart is a fracture that cannot be mended. What a fine young man you were, when the fluttering wild doves moaned with the moaning of mad love in the bewildered oryx! And the horses stumble over the heroes, grim-faced, like wolves that leap and wallow in the dust.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pearls (simile for tears rolling on cheeks)
rolling down, descending
obscured, hidden, overshadowed
a crack/fracture that cannot be set or healed
a fluttering dove; one who flaps its wings
mad with grief or love, bereaved
wolves (plural of سرحان)

context: A brief elegy comparing Sakhr to the luminous crescent moon and concluding with images of battlefield carnage, the grief of bereaved doves mirroring Al-Khansa's own ceaseless mourning.

titleEn: O eye, pour forth abundant tears

verses.ajuz: في إثر غاد سار حد النهار
من كان من ذي رحم أو جوار

verses.sadr: إن يك هذا الدهر أودى به

translationEn: O my eye, pour forth abundant tears and weep over the awe-inspiring guardian of all that is sacred. A branch from the most generous of peoples, raised among them by every man of pure lineage. When the news of his death reached me and people openly disclosed what had been whispered in secret, I said: 'My brother! If you have taken your leave of us, you were a branch from a people noble in ancestry. How many a kindness you bestowed upon families and young orphans! How many a favour you granted to captives, shackled in bondage!' May my family be ransom for him whose bones lie gleaming among the stony ground — felled by spears and sharp-honed blades flashing like lightning among the dwellings. Whoever would weep one day for a great lord, let him weep for him with hot, scalding tears. Let the horses weep for him when they are left on the field of death on the morning of disaster. Let every man of anguish whose refuge has grown too narrow weep for him — a spring of rain in destruction, a refuge of dew when people fear drought. May the land that holds his grave be watered by the first spring rains and the rushing clouds. And my prayer for that is only so that the skulls in the wilderness may be quenched with water. Say to him who gloats over his fall: you and death are wrapped together in one cloak. What consoles my grief is that whoever rejoiced at his death will follow him — do not doubt it. Between them is but an evening's journey, following a morning traveller who set out at midday. O you who struck the horseman in battle with the sword in the heat-shimmering fray! He brought him down on the horse's neck, a sleek swift mount like a wolf, sure-footed in the charge. He fought heroes who had defenders until they turned back from the sacred bonds of honour. I swear by the House and its pilgrims as they drive their white camels towards the stone pillars: I shall never grieve for any man who has died after you, as long as the pregnant she-camels call out. O the anguish, its torment is manifest, striking sparks of grief in my heart like burning embers! Those who were kin or neighbours have shown me coldness since his departure. If fate has done away with him and reduced him to dust traced over by the running waters — yet every living thing is destined for decay, and every rope of strength must one day unravel.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: awe-inspiring, one who strikes with wonder
guardian of what is sacred and inviolable
captives, prisoners (plural of عانٍ)
shackled, bound tightly in captivity
the first spring rains (the most nourishing)
torments of grief, agonies of passion
unravelling, disintegration, being buried and forgotten

context: One of Al-Khansa's most extended and philosophically rich elegies, moving from personal grief to consolation through the universality of death, and containing a remarkable sworn oath that she will mourn no other after Sakhr. The poem ends on a stoic note about the inevitability of decay.

titleEn: O Sakhr, who will face fate's trials
translationEn: O Sakhr! Who now is there for the trials of fate? Or who will make easy the path of the one riding through rough terrain? You were the one who relieved whatever afflicted us — yet now I find no sweetness and no bitterness from you. Dust is heaped upon his fair features and upon the freshness of his radiant face.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the accidents and misfortunes of fate
the rider of rough, difficult terrain
the one who brings relief, who eases hardship
whatever afflicts, whatever calamity befalls
you neither give sweetness nor give milk — proverbial for producing nothing
freshness, bloom, youthfulness

context: A very short but striking fragment mourning Sakhr's irreplaceable role as the tribe's problem-solver, using the proverbial phrase 'neither sweet nor bitter' to convey the emptiness left by his death.

titleEn: You called on Amir but cast him aside
translationEn: You called on Amir but then abandoned him, and you did not call on Muawiya ibn Amr. Had you called out to him, he would have come to you running, swift in his gallop, or he would have come at full stride — proud when the spears clashed and crossed, avenging every injury with a counterstrike. When he met death, he did not care whether it came to him in ease or in hardship, like a lion crouching with its forepaws spread, bold-chested, a great stealthy hunter.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you cast him aside, abandoned him
swift in running, at a rapid pace
proud, self-assured, striding boldly
when the spear-shafts clash and interlock
crouching with forepaws spread (of a lion ready to pounce)
a great, cunning hunter (said of a lion)
stretched out, stalking with long strides

context: This elegy mourns Muawiya ibn Amr, Al-Khansa's other brother, criticising the tribe for turning to the wrong man for help while this fearless warrior was available. It is one of the few poems primarily centred on Muawiya rather than Sakhr.

titleEn: We were like night stars with a moon among them

verses.ajuz: يجلو الدجى فهوى من بيننا القمر

verses.sadr: يا صخر ! ما كنت في قوم أسر بهم
فاذهب حميدا على ما كان من حدث

translationEn: We were like the stars of the night with a moon at their centre, dispelling darkness — and then the moon fell from among us. O Sakhr! Whenever you were among a people that delighted me, you were always the one most famed among them. Depart then, praised for all that came to pass, for you have trodden a path that holds a lesson for all.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: dispelling the darkness (of night)
fell, plummeted (the moon falling from the sky)
renowned, famous, the one known to all
praised, honoured, lauded
a lesson, a source of moral reflection

context: A brief but gem-like elegy using the central image of a moon falling from among stars — one of the most memorable similes in Al-Khansa's poetry — to convey Sakhr's irreplaceable centrality to his tribe.

titleEn: We were like two branches of a tall tree

verses.ajuz: حينا على خير ما ينمى له الشجر

verses.sadr: حتى اذا قيل قد طالت عروقهما
أخنى على واحد ريب الزمان ، وما
verses.ajuz: يبقي الزمان على شيء ولا يذر

translationEn: We were like two branches growing tall from a single root, for a time in the finest growth a tree could produce. Until it was said that their roots had grown deep and long, and their planting had ripened, and the fruit had rounded fully — then fate's calamity fell upon one of them. For time spares nothing and leaves nothing untouched.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the root stock, the base of a tree
tall and lofty, high-growing (of a tree or branch)
the fruit gathered and rounded, becoming complete
brought ruin and destruction (said of fate or time)
the treacheries and misfortunes of time

context: A philosophical mini-elegy using the image of two flourishing branches cut down by time's cruelty, ending with one of Al-Khansa's most quoted observations on the indiscriminate destruction wrought by fate.

titleAr: يا عين جودي بدمع منك مدرار
titleEn: O eye, pour forth streaming tears

verses.sadr: يا عين جودي بدمع منك مدرار

verses.ajuz: كالبدر يجلو ولا يخفى على الساري

translationEn: O my eye, be generous with streaming tears, straining in lamentation like the water of a running stream. Weep for your brother and forget not his qualities, weep for your brother who was brave and never craven. Weep for your brother — for the orphans and the widow, weep for your brother for the right of the guest and the neighbour. His generosity was abundant, his fingertips always moist with giving; like the full moon he shone and was never hidden from the night traveller. A returner of loans and a ransomer of captive women, like a ferocious lion, crushing its opponent. A crosser of valleys, a bearer of banners in war, open-handed and generous without stint.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pouring incessantly, torrential
loud lamentation, wailing in grief
one who returns borrowed items faithfully
one who ransoms captive women
a lion (a poetic epithet)
one who seizes and crushes, a crusher (of an opponent)
a bearer of battle standards

context: A catalogue elegy enumerating Sakhr's virtues in parallel phrases — his generosity, bravery, care for the weak, and prowess in battle — each verse adding another dimension to the portrait of the ideal warrior-chieftain.

titleAr: الا ابكي على صخر وصخر ثمالنا
titleEn: Weep for Sakhr, our mainstay in battle

verses.sadr: الا ابكي على صخر وصخر ثمالنا
من الهضبة العليا التي ليس كالصفا

verses.ajuz: منيع الذرى عال على من يثيرها
إذا مل عنها ذات يوم ضجورها
به عن حيال ملقح من يبورها

translationEn: Weep for Sakhr — and Sakhr was our mainstay whenever war bared its fangs and its bitterness ran strong. He steadied war's two wings and they joined forces against its hard blows until even its most difficult aspects were set right, in a flashing engagement where death dwelt in a cloud of dust, its shoulders and its throats envenomed. He rushed upon it while blood was flowing and its thunder was the murmur of heroes seldom slackening. For Sakhr in its midst was the speaker for all of war, and Sakhr when men betrayed, he swept it into flight. From the highest plateau whose smooth stone is unlike any other flatness, and whose boulders are unlike any other boulders — it has battlements none can reach and an impregnable shoulder, lofty in its peaks, towering over any who might try to stir it. His were two expanses of glory: one hand that gave abundantly, and another whose spear-tips were its jagged ridges. War had reared him and he did not tire of it, even when the weary grew disgusted and turned away. When war clouded dark for the night assault, it was certain through him, and from his firm seat it inseminated whoever was barren of it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: our support, our mainstay, our refuge
